name: France AME — Aide Médicale de l'État (State Medical Aid)
organization: French Ministry of Health (Ministère de la Santé)
description: The Aide Médicale de l'État (AME) is France's publicly funded healthcare program providing free medical coverage to undocumented immigrants who have resided in France for at least three months and whose income falls below a designated threshold. AME covers hospital and outpatient care, prescription medications, maternity care, dental treatment, and preventive services. Updated in February 2026, applicants now require proof of uninterrupted three-month residency and a tax identity number. The program covers an estimated 400,000 beneficiaries annually and remains one of Europe's most comprehensive healthcare safety nets for irregular migrants.
eligibility: Undocumented immigrants residing in France for at least 3 consecutive months with annual income below the designated threshold (currently around €9,700 for a single person).
amount: Full medical coverage (free at point of care)
applicationProcess: Submit an application at the local CPAM (Caisse Primaire d'Assurance Maladie) office with proof of identity, three months of residency documentation, income declaration, and a recent photograph. Processing takes approximately two months.
geographicScope: France — All mainland departments and overseas territories
documentsRequired: Proof of identity (passport or consular document), proof of 3+ months residency in France, income declaration, tax identity number (if available), recent biometric photograph
targetDiagnosis: General — all medical conditions including hospitalization, prescriptions, maternity, dental, and preventive care

name: Germany Gesundheitskarte für Flüchtlinge (Health Card for Refugees)
organization: German Federal Government — Asylum Seekers Benefits Act (AsylbLG)
description: The Gesundheitskarte für Flüchtlinge (Health Card for Refugees) provides asylum seekers and tolerated persons in Germany with direct access to healthcare services. During the first 36 months, coverage is limited to acute illnesses, pain management, pregnancy and maternity care, vaccinations, and preventive checkups. After 36 months, refugees receive the electronic health card (eGK) granting access to the full range of statutory health insurance benefits, including specialist care, mental health services, chronic disease management, and rehabilitative treatments. The card eliminates the previous voucher-based system, allowing direct doctor visits.
eligibility: Asylum seekers with a pending application, tolerated persons (Duldung holders), and recognized refugees in Germany. Full coverage after 36 months of residence; limited emergency and acute care coverage during the initial period.
applicationProcess: Healthcare access is arranged through the local Sozialamt (social welfare office) during the asylum registration process. In municipalities using the electronic health card, you receive the card directly; otherwise, obtain treatment vouchers (Behandlungsscheine) from the Sozialamt before visiting a doctor.
geographicScope: Germany — All 16 federal states (implementation varies by municipality)
documentsRequired: Registration certificate (Meldebescheinigung), proof of asylum application or toleration status (Aufenthaltsgestattung or Duldung), social welfare office registration
targetDiagnosis: First 36 months: acute illnesses, pain, pregnancy, vaccinations, preventive care. After 36 months: full statutory health insurance coverage including specialist, mental health, and rehabilitation services

name: UK NHS Healthcare for Refugees & Asylum Seekers
description: Refugees and asylum seekers in the United Kingdom are entitled to free NHS healthcare across all four UK nations. This includes GP registration and consultations, hospital treatment, mental health services, maternity care, and prescriptions. Asylum seekers with an active claim or appeal, refugees with leave to remain, and those on Section 95 or Section 4 support are fully exempt from NHS charges. A&E treatment is free for everyone regardless of immigration status. The NHS also provides specialized refugee health services including trauma-informed care, tuberculosis screening, and infectious disease testing upon arrival.
eligibility: Refugees with leave to remain, asylum seekers with a pending application or appeal, and those receiving Section 95 or Section 4 Home Office support. Failed asylum seekers receiving Section 4 support also qualify.
applicationProcess: Register with a local GP surgery by visiting in person. GP practices cannot refuse registration based on immigration status, lack of fixed address, or absence of ID. For hospital services, present proof of asylum/refugee status. No referral needed for A&E.
geographicScope: United Kingdom — England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland (rules vary slightly by nation)
documentsRequired: Proof of asylum claim (ARC card, IS96 form, or Home Office correspondence) or refugee status documentation. GP registration does not legally require proof of address or ID, though practices may request it.
targetDiagnosis: General — full NHS coverage including GP visits, hospital treatment, mental health, maternity, dental (with exemptions), prescriptions, and specialist referrals

name: UK NHS Low Income Scheme (LIS) — Help with Health Costs
description: The NHS Low Income Scheme (LIS) provides financial assistance with healthcare costs to anyone living in England, regardless of immigration status, whose income falls below a certain level. The scheme covers or partially covers prescription charges, dental treatment costs, sight tests and glasses/contact lenses, travel costs to hospital for NHS treatment, and NHS wigs and fabric supports. Applicants receive an HC2 certificate (full help) or HC3 certificate (partial help) valid for up to 12 months. This is a critical resource for immigrants, refugees, and asylum seekers who face medical costs not covered by other exemptions.
eligibility: Any person living in England on a low income, regardless of immigration status. Income thresholds are assessed based on household size and savings.
amount: Full or partial coverage of prescription charges, dental fees, optical costs, and travel to hospital
applicationProcess: Complete form HC1 (available from Jobcentre Plus, NHS hospitals, or online at nhsbsa.nhs.uk). Submit by post to NHSBSA. Assessment takes approximately 4 weeks. If eligible, you receive an HC2 or HC3 certificate.
geographicScope: England, United Kingdom (Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland have separate schemes with broader free provision)
documentsRequired: Completed HC1 form, details of income and savings, proof of housing costs. No immigration documents required.
targetDiagnosis: General — covers prescriptions, dental, optical, and travel costs for all medical conditions
ageRange: All ages (under-16s and over-60s already receive many exemptions)

name: FDA Orphan Products Clinical Trials Grants Program
description: The FDA Orphan Products Clinical Trials Grants Program provides funding for clinical studies of products (drugs, biologics, devices, and medical foods) intended to treat rare diseases affecting fewer than 200,000 people in the United States. The program supports Phase 1–3 clinical trials and funds both domestic and international investigators. Grants cover up to 4 years and $600,000 per year in direct costs. This program is relevant to immigrants with rare diseases who may benefit from experimental treatments, and to international researchers studying conditions prevalent in migrant populations. The program accepts new applications annually.
eligibility: Academic institutions, nonprofit organizations, and for-profit companies conducting clinical trials for rare disease treatments. International sites may participate as sub-awardees. Patients of any nationality may participate in funded trials.
amount: Up to $600,000 per year in direct costs (up to 4 years)
applicationProcess: Submit applications through NIH eRA Commons during the annual funding opportunity announcement window (typically posted on grants.gov). Applications are peer-reviewed by an FDA advisory committee.
geographicScope: United States — with international sub-awardee sites permitted
documentsRequired: FDA IND/IDE application number, clinical trial protocol, IRB/ethics approval, investigator credentials, budget justification, institutional support documentation
targetDiagnosis: Rare diseases (prevalence < 200,000 in the US) — includes rare genetic disorders, rare cancers, orphan conditions with limited treatment options
ageRange: All ages (varies by individual clinical trial protocol)

name: MRFF 2026 International Clinical Trial Collaborations Grant
organization: National Health and Medical Research Council (NHMRC) — Australia
description: The Medical Research Future Fund (MRFF) 2026 International Clinical Trial Collaborations Grant Opportunity supports Australian-led participation in multinational clinical trials. The program aims to increase clinical trial activity in Australia and strengthen international research partnerships. Grants fund investigator-initiated clinical trials addressing unmet health needs, including conditions affecting migrant and refugee populations. Both Australian and international partner sites are supported. The program prioritizes trials with translational impact that can improve health outcomes across diverse populations.
eligibility: Australian universities, medical research institutes, and hospitals leading international clinical trial collaborations. International partner organizations may receive sub-funding.
applicationProcess: Apply through the NHMRC Grant Tracker portal during the open application window. Applications require a detailed clinical trial protocol, partnership agreements with international sites, budget, and ethics approvals.
geographicScope: Australia — with international partner sites across multiple countries
documentsRequired: NHMRC Grant Tracker account, clinical trial protocol, ethics committee approval, partnership agreements with international collaborators, institutional endorsement
targetDiagnosis: Broad — includes conditions with unmet treatment needs, chronic diseases, and health challenges in diverse and underserved populations
ageRange: All ages (determined by individual trial protocols)

name: Doctors of the World UK — Free Healthcare for Migrants
organization: Doctors of the World UK (Médecins du Monde)
description: Doctors of the World UK operates free clinics and mobile health services for people facing barriers to accessing NHS care, including undocumented migrants, refused asylum seekers, victims of trafficking, and homeless individuals. Services include primary care consultations, sexual and reproductive health, mental health support, chronic disease management, and assistance with NHS registration. Their network of volunteer doctors, nurses, and interpreters provides culturally sensitive care in multiple languages. The organization also runs advocacy programs to help patients overcome barriers to mainstream NHS services and offers a telephone advice line for healthcare navigation.
eligibility: Anyone unable to access mainstream NHS services, including undocumented migrants, refused asylum seekers, victims of trafficking, EU nationals facing barriers, and people experiencing homelessness.
applicationProcess: Walk in to any Doctors of the World clinic during opening hours, or call their telephone advice line. No appointment, referral, immigration documents, or proof of address required.
geographicScope: United Kingdom — clinics in London (Bethnal Green, Hackney), with remote consultations available nationwide
documentsRequired: None required — services are provided regardless of documentation status
targetDiagnosis: General — primary care, sexual health, mental health, chronic disease management, maternity care, and NHS registration assistance
